@graph.@id: https://pinnacleblooms.org/intervention-techniques/sensory processing / self-regulation / behavioral safety / nervous system support/self-injurious behavior / sensory seeking / proprioceptive input / interoception / crisis prevention/9-materials-that-help-with-sensory-based-self-injury#faq

mainEntity.name: Why does my daughter hits her head against walls. not gently — with force, repeatedly, until i'm terrified she'll cause real damage. she bites her own hands until they bleed, leaving marks that take weeks to heal. she slaps her face hard enough to leave red marks. she pulls her hair out in clumps. she digs her nails into her skin until she breaks it. every time it happens, my heart shatters. i feel helpless, terrified, desperate. i've tried everything i can think of to make her stop. i've held her hands to prevent the hitting. i've said 'no' firmly, gently, desperately. i've tried to remove her from whatever situation seems to trigger it. i've offered distractions, comfort, alternatives. nothing works consistently. and sometimes — this is the hardest part — trying to stop her makes it worse. my intervention becomes another overwhelming input, and the behavior escalates. so i'm caught between watching her hurt herself and making it worse by trying to help. i've started to notice patterns. it happens more when she's overwhelmed — when there's too much noise, too many people, too many demands, too much happening around her. her system gets flooded, and she starts hitting. but it also happens in the opposite situation — when things are too quiet, too still, when she seems almost understimulated or bored. she'll be sitting calmly, and then suddenly she's biting her hand. the pediatrician referred us to an occupational therapist who said it might be sensory. that my daughter isn't trying to hurt herself — she's seeking input her nervous system desperately needs. the hitting provides proprioceptive feedback. the biting gives her intense oral input and pressure. the head-banging creates vestibular and deep pressure input that somehow organizes her system. the pain isn't punishment or distress expression — it's regulation. her body is trying to feel itself, to organize chaotic sensory information, to create input intense enough to register in a system that may be under-responsive. she's trying to cope. this reframe helped me understand, but it doesn't stop the terror i feel watching her hurt herself. understanding that it's sensory doesn't make the wounds heal faster. knowing it's regulatory doesn't make it safe. i can't just let it happen and watch her injure herself. but i can't stop it through willpower, commands, or physical prevention either. i need something else. i need materials that give her body what it's looking for without the damage. i need input that's intense enough to meet the need, safe enough to not cause harm, and accessible enough that she can reach for it instead of reaching for self-injury. i need to give her nervous system what it's seeking through a pathway that doesn't hurt her. because she's not trying to hurt herself. she's trying to feel okay. she's trying to regulate a system that won't regulate on its own. and i need to help her find a safer way to get there?

acceptedAnswer.text: SENSORY-BASED SELF-INJURIOUS BEHAVIOR (SIB) / AUTOMATICALLY-REINFORCED SELF-INJURY / PROPRIOCEPTIVE-SEEKING SIB / SENSORY REGULATION THROUGH SELF-INJURY! Sensory-based self-injurious behavior occurs when a child engages in actions that cause physical harm to their own body — such as head-banging, self-biting, hitting, scratching, or hair-pulling — to obtain sensory input that their nervous system requires for regulation. Unlike self-injury driven by communication (escape or attention functions), sensory-based SIB is automatically reinforced: the behavior itself produces the sensory consequence the nervous system seeks. This may include proprioceptive input (deep pressure, joint compression), vestibular input (head movement, impact), oral-motor input (biting pressure), or tactile input (scratching, pinching). The child is not trying to cause pain or injury — they are trying to regulate a nervous system that is either under-responsive (requiring intense input to register sensation) or over-responsive (using intense input to override overwhelming sensory chaos). Treatment focuses on providing alternative sensory input that meets the same neurological need through safer channels, while also addressing any underlying sensory processing differences, communication needs, or environmental factors.

mainEntity.name: What is Sensory-Based Self-Injurious Behavior (SIB) / Automatically-Reinforced Self-Injury / Proprioceptive-Seeking SIB / Sensory Regulation Through Self-Injury?

acceptedAnswer.text: Sensory-based self-injurious behavior occurs when a child engages in actions that cause physical harm to their own body — such as head-banging, self-biting, hitting, scratching, or hair-pulling — to obtain sensory input that their nervous system requires for regulation. Unlike self-injury driven by communication (escape or attention functions), sensory-based SIB is automatically reinforced: the behavior itself produces the sensory consequence the nervous system seeks. This may include proprioceptive input (deep pressure, joint compression), vestibular input (head movement, impact), oral-motor input (biting pressure), or tactile input (scratching, pinching). The child is not trying to cause pain or injury — they are trying to regulate a nervous system that is either under-responsive (requiring intense input to register sensation) or over-responsive (using intense input to override overwhelming sensory chaos). Treatment focuses on providing alternative sensory input that meets the same neurological need through safer channels, while also addressing any underlying sensory processing differences, communication needs, or environmental factors. Common signs include: Head-banging against walls, floors, furniture, or own body, Self-biting of hands, arms, wrists, or other accessible body parts, Hitting or slapping own face, head, or body, Scratching or pinching own skin until injured, Hair-pulling (trichotillomania-like presentation).

mainEntity.name: How does a Weighted and Compression Gear help with sensory-based self-injurious behavior (sib)?

acceptedAnswer.text: Weighted blankets, vests, lap pads, and compression clothing provide sustained, distributed proprioceptive input — the deep pressure sensation that many self-injuring children are seeking through hitting, head-banging, or pressing. Unlike the momentary, localized impact of self-injury, weighted and compression items deliver continuous, whole-body or large-area input that can satisfy the nervous system's need without causing harm. The pressure is calming to the nervous system, helping shift from dysregulated to regulated states. For children who bang their heads or hit themselves, weighted items can provide the 'heavy' sensory feedback their bodies crave. Compression vests and clothing offer portable, consistent proprioceptive input throughout the day, potentially reducing the build-up of sensory need that leads to self-injury episodes. Price: ₹1,500–8,000. DIY option: Create weighted lap pads with fabric and rice or dried beans. Use tight-fitting clothing or layer compression shirts under regular clothes. Try firm, sustained hugs with consistent pressure. Roll child tightly in blankets (with consent and safety awareness). Safety: Weighted items should generally not exceed 10% of child's body weight. Never use weighted items on a child who cannot remove them independently. Compression should be snug but not restrict breathing or circulation. Monitor for overheating.

mainEntity.name: How does a Chewy Tubes and Oral Motor Tools help with sensory-based self-injurious behavior (sib)?

acceptedAnswer.text: Children who bite themselves are often seeking intense oral-motor input — the deep proprioceptive feedback from jaw muscles working against resistance, combined with the tactile sensation in the mouth. Chewy tubes, chewable jewelry, and oral motor tools provide a safe target for this seeking. These items are designed to withstand intense chewing without breaking, delivering the jaw-muscle work and oral input the child needs without the tissue damage of self-biting. Different textures and resistance levels allow matching the tool to the child's specific seeking pattern. Some children need very firm resistance; others respond to textured surfaces. The key is providing something safe to bite that satisfies the same sensory need the self-biting was meeting. Price: ₹300–1,500. DIY option: Offer safe chewing alternatives: thick rubber tubing (food-grade), frozen washcloths, chewy foods throughout the day (dried fruit, bagels, jerky). Always supervise and ensure items are safe for mouthing and won't break into choking hazards. Safety: Inspect chewy items regularly for wear, cracks, or pieces breaking off. Replace when damaged. Ensure items are appropriate for the child's developmental level and won't pose choking risk. Some children may need supervision while using chewies.

mainEntity.name: How does a Crash Pads and Safe Impact Surfaces help with sensory-based self-injurious behavior (sib)?

acceptedAnswer.text: Children who seek input through head-banging or body-slamming are often pursuing intense proprioceptive and vestibular input that comes from whole-body impact. Crash pads, crash pillows, and dedicated safe impact surfaces allow this seeking to happen without injury. The child can throw themselves into the surface, experiencing the whole-body input of impact — the compression, the joint feedback, the vestibular sensation of falling and stopping — without hitting hard surfaces that cause damage. Crash pads are large, cushioned surfaces designed specifically for this purpose. They can be permanent fixtures in a sensory room or portable for use during high-need times. The key is making safe impact available so the child doesn't need to seek impact from walls and floors. Price: ₹3,000–15,000. DIY option: Create crash spaces using thick mattresses, piled pillows, bean bag chairs, or cushioned mats layered together. Ensure the crash area is away from hard surfaces and sharp corners. Teach the child where crashing is safe. Safety: Crash surfaces must be thick enough to absorb impact and away from walls, furniture, or hard floors. Monitor for safe crashing technique. Some children may need guidance to use crash spaces rather than walls. Ensure the crash area cannot lead to falls from height.

mainEntity.name: How does a Resistance and Heavy Work Tools help with sensory-based self-injurious behavior (sib)?

acceptedAnswer.text: Heavy work — activities that require muscles to push, pull, carry, or resist significant force — provides intense proprioceptive input through the joints and muscles throughout the body. This is the same type of input that self-hitting and head-banging attempt to generate, but delivered through functional activity rather than injury. Resistance tools include therapy bands, push toys, weighted items to carry, and equipment that requires physical effort to use. The intensity of the input can match or exceed what self-injury provides, but through productive channels. Regular heavy work throughout the day can keep the proprioceptive system satisfied, potentially preventing the build-up that leads to self-injury. When a child begins to seek impact, redirecting to heavy work can meet the need immediately. Price: ₹500–5,000. DIY option: Create heavy work opportunities: carrying groceries or laundry, pushing furniture (safely), wheelbarrow walking, animal walks (bear walk, crab walk), wall push-ups, pulling wagons with weight, playing tug-of-war. Make heavy work a regular part of daily routine. Safety: Match resistance levels to child's strength and ability. Avoid excessive strain that could cause injury. Supervise use of resistance equipment. Some children may need physical guidance to use tools effectively.

mainEntity.name: How does a Vibration Tools and Massagers help with sensory-based self-injurious behavior (sib)?

acceptedAnswer.text: Vibration provides intense, rapidly oscillating proprioceptive and tactile input that can satisfy or override the sensory seeking that drives self-injury. Vibration penetrates deeply into tissues, stimulating proprioceptors in muscles and joints in a way that surface touch cannot. For some children, vibration is more organizing than even deep pressure — the oscillating input captures attention and floods the sensory system in a regulating rather than dysregulating way. Vibrating tools can be applied to the body areas the child typically targets in self-injury, providing intense input to those specific proprioceptors. Vibrating oral-motor tools can substitute for self-biting. Whole-body vibration platforms provide systemic input that can organize the entire nervous system. Price: ₹500–4,000. DIY option: Use electric massagers from general retail on safe body areas. Battery-operated toothbrushes provide oral vibration. Create vibrating experiences through massage, vibrating pillows, or vibrating chairs. Always test on yourself first and monitor child's response. Safety: Avoid vibration near eyes, throat, or other vulnerable areas. Some children find vibration dysregulating rather than calming — observe response carefully. Don't force vibration on children who resist it. Check equipment for safety and appropriate intensity.

mainEntity.name: How does a Protective Gear and Padding help with sensory-based self-injurious behavior (sib)?

acceptedAnswer.text: While the primary goal is providing alternative input that replaces self-injury, protective gear serves as an essential safety measure during the transition or during high-intensity episodes. Helmets protect the head during head-banging. Arm guards and gloves can prevent biting from breaking skin. Padding on targeted body areas reduces injury from hitting or scratching. Protective gear does not stop the behavior — it reduces harm while the behavior is being addressed through other means. This is harm reduction: acknowledging that the behavior may continue while intervention takes effect, and minimizing injury in the meantime. Protective gear should be paired with sensory alternatives, not used as a sole solution. The goal is to eventually not need the protection because the alternative input has replaced the self-injury. Price: ₹1,000–8,000. DIY option: Use soft sports helmets for head protection. Repurpose athletic padding for arm or body protection. Long sleeves and pants can reduce access for scratching. Ensure protection doesn't restrict movement excessively or cause overheating. Safety: Protective gear is not a substitute for intervention — it's harm reduction while intervention develops. Monitor for attempts to remove or circumvent protection. Ensure gear doesn't restrict breathing, circulation, or cause overheating. Balance protection with dignity and developmental appropriateness.

@graph.name: How to Help a Child with Sensory Based Self Injurious Behavior (sib)
@graph.description: A graded approach using evidence-based materials. Sensory-based self-injury is not a choice, defiance, or manipulation. It is a nervous system using the only available tool to achieve sensory input needed for regulation. The child is not trying to hurt themselves — they are trying to feel okay. Treatment must provide alternative pathways that meet the same sensory need without causing harm. This requires understanding what specific sensory input the self-injury is seeking (proprioceptive, vestibular, tactile, oral-motor) and providing sufficient intensity of that input through safer channels. Punishment, restraint, or simply blocking the behavior without providing alternatives will fail and may increase distress. The goal is replacement, not elimination — giving the nervous system what it needs through pathways that protect the body.

step.text: Sensory-based self-injurious behavior occurs when a child engages in actions that cause physical harm to their own body — such as head-banging, self-biting, hitting, scratching, or hair-pulling — to obtain sensory input that their nervous system requires for regulation. Unlike self-injury driven by communication (escape or attention functions), sensory-based SIB is automatically reinforced: the behavior itself produces the sensory consequence the nervous system seeks. This may include proprioceptive input (deep pressure, joint compression), vestibular input (head movement, impact), oral-motor input (biting pressure), or tactile input (scratching, pinching). The child is not trying to cause pain or injury — they are trying to regulate a nervous system that is either under-responsive (requiring intense input to register sensation) or over-responsive (using intense input to override overwhelming sensory chaos). Treatment focuses on providing alternative sensory input that meets the same neurological need through safer channels, while also addressing any underlying sensory processing differences, communication needs, or environmental factors. citation.name: Research on Sensory-Based Self-Injurious Behavior
citation.description: Research on automatically reinforced self-injurious behavior demonstrates that SIB maintained by sensory consequences requires sensory-based interventions — providing alternative, safer sources of the same sensory input. Functional analysis distinguishing sensory SIB from socially-mediated SIB is essential for effective treatment. Occupational therapy approaches addressing sensory processing and providing sensory diets show efficacy for sensory-maintained SIB.

citation.name: Pinnacle Blooms Network® Clinical Data
citation.description: 20M+ exclusive 1:1 therapy sessions with 97%+ measured improvement rate via GPT-OS® therapeutic system, including sensory regulation and behavioral safety outcomes tracking

citation.name: Occupational Therapy and Sensory Integration Research
citation.description: OT sensory integration approaches for children with self-injury demonstrate efficacy of sensory diets, alternative sensory input, and environmental modifications in reducing SIB

citation.name: Applied Behavior Analysis Research on SIB
citation.description: ABA research on functional analysis and function-based treatment of SIB supports the importance of determining behavioral function before selecting intervention strategies

@graph.name: 9 Materials for Sensory Based Self Injurious Behavior (sib)

item.name: Weighted and Compression Gear
item.description: Weighted blankets, vests, lap pads, and compression clothing provide sustained, distributed proprioceptive input — the deep pressure sensation that many self-injuring children are seeking through hitting, head-banging, or pressing. Unlike the momentary, localized impact of self-injury, weighted and compression items deliver continuous, whole-body or large-area input that can satisfy the nervous system's need without causing harm. The pressure is calming to the nervous system, helping shift from dysregulated to regulated states. For children who bang their heads or hit themselves, weighted items can provide the 'heavy' sensory feedback their bodies crave. Compression vests and clothing offer portable, consistent proprioceptive input throughout the day, potentially reducing the build-up of sensory need that leads to self-injury episodes.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create weighted lap pads with fabric and rice or dried beans. Use tight-fitting clothing or layer compression shirts under regular clothes. Try firm, sustained hugs with consistent pressure. Roll child tightly in blankets (with consent and safety awareness).

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Weighted items should generally not exceed 10% of child's body weight. Never use weighted items on a child who cannot remove them independently. Compression should be snug but not restrict breathing or circulation. Monitor for overheating.

item.name: Chewy Tubes and Oral Motor Tools
item.description: Children who bite themselves are often seeking intense oral-motor input — the deep proprioceptive feedback from jaw muscles working against resistance, combined with the tactile sensation in the mouth. Chewy tubes, chewable jewelry, and oral motor tools provide a safe target for this seeking. These items are designed to withstand intense chewing without breaking, delivering the jaw-muscle work and oral input the child needs without the tissue damage of self-biting. Different textures and resistance levels allow matching the tool to the child's specific seeking pattern. Some children need very firm resistance; others respond to textured surfaces. The key is providing something safe to bite that satisfies the same sensory need the self-biting was meeting.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Offer safe chewing alternatives: thick rubber tubing (food-grade), frozen washcloths, chewy foods throughout the day (dried fruit, bagels, jerky). Always supervise and ensure items are safe for mouthing and won't break into choking hazards.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Inspect chewy items regularly for wear, cracks, or pieces breaking off. Replace when damaged. Ensure items are appropriate for the child's developmental level and won't pose choking risk. Some children may need supervision while using chewies.

item.name: Crash Pads and Safe Impact Surfaces
item.description: Children who seek input through head-banging or body-slamming are often pursuing intense proprioceptive and vestibular input that comes from whole-body impact. Crash pads, crash pillows, and dedicated safe impact surfaces allow this seeking to happen without injury. The child can throw themselves into the surface, experiencing the whole-body input of impact — the compression, the joint feedback, the vestibular sensation of falling and stopping — without hitting hard surfaces that cause damage. Crash pads are large, cushioned surfaces designed specifically for this purpose. They can be permanent fixtures in a sensory room or portable for use during high-need times. The key is making safe impact available so the child doesn't need to seek impact from walls and floors.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create crash spaces using thick mattresses, piled pillows, bean bag chairs, or cushioned mats layered together. Ensure the crash area is away from hard surfaces and sharp corners. Teach the child where crashing is safe.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Crash surfaces must be thick enough to absorb impact and away from walls, furniture, or hard floors. Monitor for safe crashing technique. Some children may need guidance to use crash spaces rather than walls. Ensure the crash area cannot lead to falls from height.

item.name: Resistance and Heavy Work Tools
item.description: Heavy work — activities that require muscles to push, pull, carry, or resist significant force — provides intense proprioceptive input through the joints and muscles throughout the body. This is the same type of input that self-hitting and head-banging attempt to generate, but delivered through functional activity rather than injury. Resistance tools include therapy bands, push toys, weighted items to carry, and equipment that requires physical effort to use. The intensity of the input can match or exceed what self-injury provides, but through productive channels. Regular heavy work throughout the day can keep the proprioceptive system satisfied, potentially preventing the build-up that leads to self-injury. When a child begins to seek impact, redirecting to heavy work can meet the need immediately.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create heavy work opportunities: carrying groceries or laundry, pushing furniture (safely), wheelbarrow walking, animal walks (bear walk, crab walk), wall push-ups, pulling wagons with weight, playing tug-of-war. Make heavy work a regular part of daily routine.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Match resistance levels to child's strength and ability. Avoid excessive strain that could cause injury. Supervise use of resistance equipment. Some children may need physical guidance to use tools effectively.

item.name: Vibration Tools and Massagers
item.description: Vibration provides intense, rapidly oscillating proprioceptive and tactile input that can satisfy or override the sensory seeking that drives self-injury. Vibration penetrates deeply into tissues, stimulating proprioceptors in muscles and joints in a way that surface touch cannot. For some children, vibration is more organizing than even deep pressure — the oscillating input captures attention and floods the sensory system in a regulating rather than dysregulating way. Vibrating tools can be applied to the body areas the child typically targets in self-injury, providing intense input to those specific proprioceptors. Vibrating oral-motor tools can substitute for self-biting. Whole-body vibration platforms provide systemic input that can organize the entire nervous system.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Use electric massagers from general retail on safe body areas. Battery-operated toothbrushes provide oral vibration. Create vibrating experiences through massage, vibrating pillows, or vibrating chairs. Always test on yourself first and monitor child's response.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Avoid vibration near eyes, throat, or other vulnerable areas. Some children find vibration dysregulating rather than calming — observe response carefully. Don't force vibration on children who resist it. Check equipment for safety and appropriate intensity.

item.name: Protective Gear and Padding
item.description: While the primary goal is providing alternative input that replaces self-injury, protective gear serves as an essential safety measure during the transition or during high-intensity episodes. Helmets protect the head during head-banging. Arm guards and gloves can prevent biting from breaking skin. Padding on targeted body areas reduces injury from hitting or scratching. Protective gear does not stop the behavior — it reduces harm while the behavior is being addressed through other means. This is harm reduction: acknowledging that the behavior may continue while intervention takes effect, and minimizing injury in the meantime. Protective gear should be paired with sensory alternatives, not used as a sole solution. The goal is to eventually not need the protection because the alternative input has replaced the self-injury.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Use soft sports helmets for head protection. Repurpose athletic padding for arm or body protection. Long sleeves and pants can reduce access for scratching. Ensure protection doesn't restrict movement excessively or cause overheating.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Protective gear is not a substitute for intervention — it's harm reduction while intervention develops. Monitor for attempts to remove or circumvent protection. Ensure gear doesn't restrict breathing, circulation, or cause overheating. Balance protection with dignity and developmental appropriateness.

item.name: Fidget and Tactile Seeking Tools
item.description: Some sensory-based self-injury involves tactile seeking — the scratching, pinching, or skin-picking that provides intense tactile and proprioceptive input to the hands and skin. Fidget tools and tactile-seeking materials provide alternative input to hands and fingers, satisfying the seeking without the tissue damage. Textured fidgets, spiky balls, putty that resists squeezing, and materials that provide intense tactile feedback can redirect the hands toward safer targets. The key is matching the intensity — many standard fidgets are too mild for children whose seeking leads to self-injury. They need stronger, more intense tactile input that actually competes with what the self-injury provides.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Provide high-intensity tactile experiences: very textured materials, theraputty or stiff modeling clay, Velcro strips to rub, textured fabric scraps, ice cubes held in hands. Match the intensity of what the child is seeking.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Monitor for mouthing of fidgets. Ensure items are durable enough not to break into small pieces. Some children may need guidance to use fidgets rather than continuing to self-injure. Match intensity to need — too-mild fidgets won't compete with self-injury.

item.name: Sensory Diet and Scheduled Input
item.description: Sensory-based self-injury often occurs when the nervous system's need for input builds up over time until it becomes overwhelming, triggering the self-injury as a crisis response. A sensory diet — scheduled, proactive provision of the sensory input the child needs throughout the day — can prevent this build-up. Rather than waiting for the child to self-injure and then providing alternatives, a sensory diet delivers input regularly enough that the need never reaches crisis levels. This might include scheduled compression breaks, regular heavy work activities, planned oral-motor input times, and predictable access to calming sensory materials. The diet is designed based on the specific sensory inputs the child's self-injury suggests they need.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create a sensory diet schedule with input every 30-60 minutes. Include heavy work, compression, oral motor, and other inputs matching the child's needs. Use visual schedules to implement. Work with an occupational therapist for individualized diet development.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: A sensory diet should be developed with occupational therapy guidance to match the child's specific sensory profile. One-size-fits-all approaches may miss the child's particular needs. Monitor and adjust the diet based on whether self-injury frequency decreases.

item.name: Calming Sensory Environments
item.description: For some children, sensory-based self-injury is triggered by sensory overload — the hitting or biting begins when the nervous system is overwhelmed by environmental input and seeks intense proprioceptive input to override or organize the chaos. Creating calming sensory environments reduces the triggers for overload-related self-injury. This includes controlling lighting (dim, non-flickering), reducing noise (quiet spaces, noise-canceling headphones), minimizing visual clutter, and providing organized, predictable spaces. Designated calm spaces or sensory rooms can serve as refuges where the child can regulate before reaching the point of self-injury. The environment itself becomes a preventive intervention.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create a calm corner with controlled lighting, minimal decoration, soft surfaces. Provide noise-canceling headphones or quiet spaces. Reduce visual and auditory clutter in the child's environment. Establish retreat spaces before overload peaks.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Calming environments address overload-related self-injury but may not address under-stimulation-related self-injury. Know whether the child's self-injury is triggered by too much input (calming helps) or too little input (calming may not help). Individual assessment is essential.

socialMedia.caption: When the body hurts itself to feel regulated. Sensory-based self-injury isn't about pain — it's about input the nervous system desperately needs. Here are 9 materials that provide that input through safer pathways: weighted and compression gear, chewy tubes, crash pads, resistance and heavy work tools, vibration tools, protective gear, tactile fidgets, sensory diets, and calming environments. Not stopping the seeking — redirecting it. Meeting the need without the harm. Save to build safer sensory pathways! 🔖 #SelfInjurySupport #SensoryProcessing #PinnacleBlooms
